texto: Don't aim at success-the more you aim at it and make it a target, the more you are going to miss it. For success, like happiness, cannot be pursued; it must ensue, and it only does so as the unintended side-effect of one's dedication to a cause greater than oneself or as the by-product of one's surrender to a person other than oneself.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Victor E. Frankl: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de limites e escolhas conscientes. O trecho “Don't aim at success-the more you aim at it and make it a target, the more you are going to miss …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que motivacao, inspiracional, motivation pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de inspiracao e termino.

frase_original: Don't aim at success-the more you aim at it and make it a target, the more you are going to miss it. For success, like happiness, cannot be pursued; it must ensue, and it only does so as the unintended side-effect of one's dedication to a cause greater than oneself or as the by-product of one's surrender to a person other than oneself.

texto: Everything you have in life can be taken from you except one thing, your freedom to choose how you will respond to the situation. This is what determines the quality of the life we’ve lived — not whether we’ve been rich or poor, famous or unknown, healthy or suffering. What determines our quality of life is how we relate to these realities, what kind of meaning we assign them, what kind of attitude we cling to about them, what state of mind we allow them to trigger.
explicacao: Citação de Victor E. Frankl sobre sentido da existência e experiência vivida. “Everything you have in life can be taken from you except one thing, your freedom to choose how yo…” articula reflexao, freedom, determines em registro de reflexao, com relevância para contextos como reflexao.

frase_original: Everything you have in life can be taken from you except one thing, your freedom to choose how you will respond to the situation. This is what determines the quality of the life we’ve lived — not whether we’ve been rich or poor, famous or unknown, healthy or suffering. What determines our quality of life is how we relate to these realities, what kind of meaning we assign them, what kind of attitude we cling to about them, what state of mind we allow them to trigger.

texto: The greatest task for any person is to find meaning in his or her life. Frankl saw three possible sources for meaning: in work (doing something significant), in love (caring for another person), and in courage during difficult times. Suffering in and of itself is meaningless; we give our suffering meaning by the way in which we respond to it.
